skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 1: Basic Economic Concepts (Topic 1.1-1.6)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Basic Economic Concepts for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 1: Basic Economic Concepts, Topic 1.1-1.6). Students should understand the foundational principles that underpin all economic analysis. Key concepts: scarcity (unlimited wants, limited resources) is the fundamental economic problem; opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative forgone; the production possibilities curve (PPC) illustrates trade-offs, efficiency, and economic growth; comparative advantage determines who should produce what (produce where opportunity cost is lowest); factors of production (land, labor, capital, entrepreneurship); economic systems (market, command, mixed) differ in how they answer the three fundamental questions (what, how, for whom to produce). Students should construct and interpret PPC graphs, calculate opportunity costs, determine comparative advantage from production data, and understand how specialization and trade benefit all parties. Vocabulary: scarcity, opportunity cost, PPC, trade-off, comparative advantage, absolute advantage, factors of production, specialization, marginal analysis.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 2: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle (Topic 2.1-2.3)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Measuring Economic Performance for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 2: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle, Topic 2.1-2.3). Students should understand how economists measure the overall health and output of an economy. Key concepts: Gross Domestic Product (GDP) measures the total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a year; the expenditure approach (GDP = C + I + G + Xn); the income approach; nominal GDP vs. real GDP (adjusted for inflation using a price index); GDP deflator and CPI (Consumer Price Index) measure the overall price level; real GDP per capita as a measure of living standards; limitations of GDP (excludes non-market production, underground economy, environmental degradation, and income distribution). Students should calculate GDP, convert between nominal and real GDP, compute inflation rates using price indices, and evaluate GDP as a measure of well-being. Vocabulary: GDP, nominal GDP, real GDP, GDP deflator, CPI, inflation, final goods, intermediate goods, expenditure approach, base year.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 3: National Income and Price Determination (Topic 3.1-3.7)
skills.prompt: This topic covers National Income and Price Determination for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 3: National Income and Price Determination, Topic 3.1-3.7). Students should understand the aggregate demand and aggregate supply model that determines the price level and real GDP. Key concepts: aggregate demand (AD) shows the inverse relationship between the price level and real GDP demanded; AD shifts due to changes in consumer spending, investment, government spending, and net exports; short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) shows the positive relationship between the price level and real GDP supplied; SRAS shifts due to changes in input prices, productivity, and supply shocks; long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) is vertical at the full-employment level of output; macroeconomic equilibrium occurs where AD intersects SRAS; recessionary and inflationary gaps. Students should graph and shift AD, SRAS, and LRAS curves, identify equilibrium, and analyze how shocks affect output and prices. Vocabulary: aggregate demand, aggregate supply, SRAS, LRAS, macroeconomic equilibrium, recessionary gap, inflationary gap, price level, real GDP, potential output.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 3: National Income and Price Determination (Topic 3.2-3.5)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Aggregate Supply and Aggregate Demand for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 3: National Income and Price Determination, Topic 3.2-3.5). Students should understand how changes in aggregate demand and supply affect macroeconomic outcomes. Key concepts: demand-pull inflation occurs when AD increases, raising both the price level and real GDP in the short run; cost-push inflation occurs when SRAS decreases, raising the price level while reducing real GDP (stagflation); the multiplier effect amplifies changes in spending (spending multiplier = 1/(1-MPC) = 1/MPS); the tax multiplier is smaller than the spending multiplier (tax multiplier = -MPC/MPS); the balanced budget multiplier equals 1; the self-correcting mechanism: in the long run, wages and prices adjust to restore full employment. Students should use the AD-AS model to analyze the effects of demand and supply shocks, calculate multipliers, and understand both short-run and long-run adjustments. Vocabulary: demand-pull inflation, cost-push inflation, stagflation, multiplier effect, MPC, MPS, self-correction, wage adjustment, spending multiplier, tax multiplier.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 3: National Income and Price Determination (Topic 3.6-3.7)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Fiscal Policy for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 3: National Income and Price Determination, Topic 3.6-3.7). Students should understand how the government uses taxing and spending to influence macroeconomic outcomes. Key concepts: expansionary fiscal policy (increased government spending or decreased taxes) shifts AD right to combat recession; contractionary fiscal policy (decreased government spending or increased taxes) shifts AD left to combat inflation; automatic stabilizers (progressive income taxes, transfer payments like unemployment insurance) automatically counteract economic fluctuations without new legislation; the crowding-out effect (government borrowing raises interest rates, reducing private investment, partially offsetting fiscal stimulus); time lags (recognition lag, legislative lag, implementation lag) reduce fiscal policy effectiveness; supply-side economics argues tax cuts increase LRAS by incentivizing work and investment. Students should graph fiscal policy effects using AD-AS and explain crowding out. Vocabulary: fiscal policy, expansionary, contractionary, automatic stabilizers, crowding out, budget deficit, surplus, government spending, taxation, multiplier.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 4: Financial Sector (Topic 4.4-4.6)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Monetary Policy for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 4: Financial Sector, Topic 4.4-4.6). Students should understand how the Federal Reserve uses monetary policy tools to influence the money supply, interest rates, and economic activity. Key concepts: the Federal Reserve's tools include open market operations (buying/selling government bonds), the discount rate (interest rate on loans to commercial banks), and the reserve requirement (fraction of deposits banks must hold); expansionary monetary policy (buying bonds, lowering rates) increases the money supply, lowers interest rates, and stimulates investment and AD; contractionary monetary policy (selling bonds, raising rates) decreases the money supply, raises interest rates, and reduces AD; the federal funds rate is the target rate for overnight interbank lending; the money market model shows how money supply and demand determine the interest rate. Students should graph the money market, show how Fed actions shift supply, and connect to AD-AS outcomes. Vocabulary: monetary policy, Federal Reserve, open market operations, discount rate, reserve requirement, money supply, federal funds rate, expansionary, contractionary, interest rate.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies (Topic 5.2)
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Phillips Curve for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies, Topic 5.2). Students should understand the relationship between inflation and unemployment. Key concepts: the short-run Phillips Curve (SRPC) shows an inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment (lower unemployment comes with higher inflation and vice versa); the long-run Phillips Curve (LRPC) is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment (NRU), indicating no long-run trade-off; the SRPC shifts when expected inflation changes or supply shocks occur; stagflation (high inflation with high unemployment) shifts the SRPC outward; when actual inflation exceeds expected inflation, unemployment falls below the NRU temporarily; in the long run, adaptive expectations adjust and the economy returns to the NRU. Students should graph both SRPC and LRPC, show how demand-side and supply-side changes affect both curves, and connect Phillips Curve analysis to AD-AS outcomes. Vocabulary: Phillips Curve, SRPC, LRPC, natural rate of unemployment, inflation expectations, stagflation, trade-off, demand-pull, cost-push, NAIRU.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies (Topic 5.6-5.7)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Economic Growth for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies, Topic 5.6-5.7). Students should understand the factors that drive long-run increases in an economy's potential output. Key concepts: economic growth is measured by increases in real GDP per capita over time; growth shifts the LRAS and PPC outward; sources of growth include increases in quantity/quality of labor (human capital through education and training), increases in physical capital (investment in machinery, infrastructure), technological progress (innovation, research and development), and improvements in institutions (property rights, rule of law, stable government); the production function relates inputs to output; diminishing returns to capital suggest that technology is the key driver of sustained growth; policies promoting growth include investment in education, R&D incentives, free trade, and infrastructure spending. Students should graph growth using PPC and LRAS shifts and evaluate growth policies. Vocabulary: economic growth, real GDP per capita, human capital, physical capital, technology, productivity, LRAS shift, PPC shift, diminishing returns, investment.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 6: Open Economy (Topic 6.1-6.2)
skills.prompt: This topic covers International Trade for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 6: Open Economy, Topic 6.1-6.2). Students should understand the benefits of international trade and the arguments for and against trade barriers. Key concepts: countries benefit from trade by specializing in goods where they have a comparative advantage (lowest opportunity cost); trade barriers include tariffs (taxes on imports), quotas (quantity limits on imports), and subsidies; arguments for free trade (lower prices, greater variety, increased efficiency, economic growth); arguments for trade barriers (protecting infant industries, national security, preventing dumping, protecting jobs); the terms of trade (the price at which countries trade) must fall between the opportunity costs of the trading partners for both to benefit. Students should calculate comparative advantage from production data, determine terms of trade, and analyze the effects of tariffs and quotas using supply and demand graphs for imported goods. Vocabulary: comparative advantage, tariff, quota, free trade, trade barrier, terms of trade, export, import, specialization, dumping, subsidy.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 6: Open Economy (Topic 6.3-6.4)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Foreign Exchange Markets for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 6: Open Economy, Topic 6.3-6.4). Students should understand how exchange rates are determined and how they affect international trade and capital flows. Key concepts: an exchange rate is the price of one currency in terms of another; in a flexible (floating) exchange rate system, currency prices are determined by supply and demand in the foreign exchange market; demand for a currency comes from foreigners wanting to buy a country's goods, services, or financial assets; supply of a currency comes from domestic residents wanting to buy foreign goods, services, or assets; appreciation (currency becomes more valuable) makes imports cheaper and exports more expensive; depreciation (currency becomes less valuable) makes imports more expensive and exports cheaper; interest rate differentials, inflation differentials, and income growth affect exchange rates. Students should graph the foreign exchange market and analyze how changes in economic conditions affect exchange rates. Vocabulary: exchange rate, appreciation, depreciation, foreign exchange market, floating rate, fixed rate, demand for currency, supply of currency, capital flow.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 6: Open Economy (Topic 6.1)
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Balance of Payments for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 6: Open Economy, Topic 6.1). Students should understand how a country records its international economic transactions. Key concepts: the balance of payments consists of the current account and the financial (capital) account; the current account records trade in goods and services, investment income, and transfers (a trade deficit means imports exceed exports); the financial account records purchases and sales of financial assets (stocks, bonds, real estate, foreign direct investment); the two accounts must balance (a current account deficit is offset by a financial account surplus and vice versa); a country with a trade deficit is a net borrower from abroad; a country with a trade surplus is a net lender. Students should understand how the accounts relate, why they must sum to zero, and how trade deficits and surpluses affect a country's international financial position. Vocabulary: balance of payments, current account, financial account, trade deficit, trade surplus, net exports, capital flows, foreign direct investment, transfer payments.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 2: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle (Topic 2.4-2.5)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Inflation and Unemployment for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 2: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle, Topic 2.4-2.5). Students should understand how these two key indicators are measured and their effects on the economy. Key concepts: unemployment rate = (unemployed/labor force) x 100; types of unemployment include frictional (between jobs), structural (skills mismatch), cyclical (due to economic downturns), and seasonal; the natural rate of unemployment (NRU) includes frictional and structural but not cyclical; full employment means zero cyclical unemployment, not zero total unemployment; inflation is a sustained increase in the general price level; CPI measures consumer prices; costs of inflation include decreased purchasing power, menu costs, shoe-leather costs, and arbitrary redistribution of wealth between borrowers and lenders; deflation can be more dangerous than moderate inflation. Students should calculate unemployment rates, identify types of unemployment, and analyze inflation's effects on different groups. Vocabulary: unemployment rate, labor force, frictional, structural, cyclical, natural rate, inflation, CPI, deflation, purchasing power, real vs. nominal.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 2: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle (Topic 2.6)
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Business Cycle for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 2: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle, Topic 2.6). Students should understand the recurring pattern of expansion and contraction in economic activity. Key concepts: the business cycle consists of four phases: expansion (growing GDP, falling unemployment), peak (maximum output before downturn), contraction/recession (declining GDP, rising unemployment), and trough (minimum output before recovery); a recession is typically defined as two consecutive quarters of declining real GDP; leading economic indicators (stock market, building permits, consumer confidence) predict future economic activity; lagging indicators (unemployment rate, CPI) confirm trends; coincident indicators (GDP, industrial production) move with the economy. Students should identify business cycle phases, connect them to changes in unemployment, inflation, and output, and understand how fiscal and monetary policy attempt to smooth the business cycle. Vocabulary: business cycle, expansion, peak, contraction, recession, trough, recovery, leading indicator, lagging indicator, GDP growth, output gap.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies (Topic 5.6)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Long-Run Economic Growth for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies, Topic 5.6). Students should understand what determines an economy's long-run growth trajectory and potential output. Key concepts: long-run growth is driven by increases in productivity (output per worker) rather than short-run demand changes; the aggregate production function shows how inputs (labor, capital, technology) combine to produce output; technological progress shifts the production function upward, allowing more output from the same inputs; physical capital accumulation is subject to diminishing returns, so technology is the key to sustained growth; policies promoting long-run growth include investing in education and human capital, encouraging savings and investment, supporting research and development, maintaining stable institutions, and promoting free trade. Students should distinguish between short-run fluctuations and long-run growth trends and evaluate growth policies. Vocabulary: long-run growth, productivity, production function, technology, physical capital, human capital, diminishing returns, potential output, per capita income.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 4: Financial Sector (Topic 4.1-4.3)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Money and Banking for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 4: Financial Sector, Topic 4.1-4.3). Students should understand the functions of money, the banking system, and how money is created. Key concepts: money serves as a medium of exchange, unit of account, and store of value; commodity money (gold) vs. fiat money (government-decreed); the money supply measures include M1 (currency + demand deposits + traveler's checks) and M2 (M1 + savings, small time deposits, money market funds); fractional reserve banking means banks hold a fraction of deposits and lend the rest; the money multiplier (1/reserve requirement) shows how the banking system creates money through the lending process; the required reserve ratio determines how much banks must hold. Students should calculate the money multiplier and trace how an initial deposit creates a multiple expansion of the money supply through the banking system. Vocabulary: money, medium of exchange, fiat money, M1, M2, fractional reserve banking, money multiplier, required reserves, excess reserves, demand deposits, money creation.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 4: Financial Sector (Topic 4.1-4.2)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Financial Markets for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 4: Financial Sector, Topic 4.1-4.2). Students should understand the loanable funds market and how it determines the real interest rate. Key concepts: the loanable funds market brings together savers (supply of loanable funds) and borrowers (demand for loanable funds); the real interest rate is determined by the intersection of supply and demand for loanable funds; supply comes from private savings, government surplus, and foreign capital inflows; demand comes from businesses investing, consumers borrowing, and government borrowing; government budget deficits increase demand for loanable funds, raising real interest rates (crowding out private investment); an increase in savings shifts supply right, lowering interest rates; the Fisher equation relates nominal interest rate, real interest rate, and expected inflation (nominal = real + expected inflation). Students should graph the loanable funds market and analyze how economic changes affect real interest rates and investment. Vocabulary: loanable funds, real interest rate, nominal interest rate, savings, investment, crowding out, Fisher equation, capital market, budget deficit, supply of loanable funds.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies (Topic 5.4-5.5)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Government Deficits and Debt for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies, Topic 5.4-5.5). Students should understand the relationship between government spending, revenue, deficits, and the accumulating national debt. Key concepts: a budget deficit occurs when government spending exceeds tax revenue in a given year; the national debt is the cumulative total of all past deficits minus surpluses; deficit spending is financed by selling government bonds; crowding out occurs when government borrowing competes with private borrowing, raising interest rates and reducing private investment; the debt-to-GDP ratio is a more meaningful measure than absolute debt level; Ricardian equivalence suggests that rational consumers save more when government borrows (anticipating future tax increases), offsetting stimulus effects; arguments about debt sustainability involve future growth rates vs. interest rates. Students should graph the crowding-out effect in the loanable funds market and evaluate arguments about the significance of government debt. Vocabulary: deficit, surplus, national debt, government bonds, crowding out, debt-to-GDP ratio, Ricardian equivalence, interest payments, fiscal sustainability.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 6: Open Economy (Topic 6.5-6.6)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Open Economy Macroeconomics for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 6: Open Economy, Topic 6.5-6.6). Students should understand how international capital flows and exchange rates interact with domestic economic policy. Key concepts: in an open economy, the current account and financial account are linked (a trade deficit is financed by capital inflows); higher domestic interest rates attract foreign capital, increasing demand for the domestic currency and causing appreciation; appreciation makes exports more expensive and imports cheaper, worsening the trade balance; this creates a potential conflict: expansionary fiscal policy raises interest rates (crowding out), attracts foreign capital, causes appreciation, and reduces net exports, partially offsetting the stimulus. Students should trace the chain of effects from fiscal and monetary policy changes through interest rates, exchange rates, and net exports, and understand how international linkages complicate domestic policy. Vocabulary: open economy, capital flows, exchange rate, appreciation, depreciation, net exports, trade balance, interest rate differential, capital account, current account.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 2: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle (Topic 2.1-2.2)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Economic Indicators for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 2: Economic Indicators and the Business Cycle, Topic 2.1-2.2). Students should understand the key statistics used to measure and evaluate economic performance. Key concepts: GDP (real and nominal) measures total output; the unemployment rate measures labor market health; the inflation rate (measured by CPI and GDP deflator) measures price stability; real GDP growth rate indicates economic expansion or contraction; GDP per capita approximates living standards; leading indicators (stock prices, building permits, consumer expectations) predict future trends; lagging indicators (average duration of unemployment, prime rate) confirm trends; coincident indicators (industrial production, personal income) track current conditions; limitations of these indicators (GDP excludes non-market activity, CPI may overstate inflation due to substitution bias, unemployment rate excludes discouraged workers). Students should interpret economic data, identify trends, and evaluate the health of an economy using multiple indicators. Vocabulary: economic indicator, GDP, unemployment rate, inflation rate, CPI, GDP deflator, leading indicator, lagging indicator, labor force participation rate, real GDP.

skills.ref: AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies (Topic 5.1-5.3)
skills.prompt: This topic covers Economic Policy Debates for AP Macroeconomics students (AP Macro Unit 5: Long-Run Consequences of Stabilization Policies, Topic 5.1-5.3). Students should understand the major debates about how best to manage the macroeconomy. Key concepts: the debate between Keynesian economics (government intervention through fiscal policy to stabilize the economy) and classical/monetarist economics (markets are self-correcting, government intervention often does more harm than good); active vs. passive policy (should government try to fine-tune the economy or follow fixed rules?); discretionary policy vs. rules-based policy; supply-side economics (tax cuts stimulate growth by increasing incentives to work, save, and invest); the debate over the effectiveness of fiscal vs. monetary policy; policy time lags (recognition, implementation, impact) reduce effectiveness; the role of expectations in policy effectiveness (rational expectations theory). Students should evaluate arguments from different economic schools of thought and apply them to real-world scenarios. Vocabulary: Keynesian, classical, monetarist, supply-side, discretionary policy, rules-based, time lag, expectations, self-correcting, stabilization policy.
